elements.sent-2411-001: Evaluating the output of Large Language Models (LLMs) is one of the most critical aspects of building a performant compound AI system.
elements.sent-2411-002: Since the output from LLMs propagate to downstream steps, identifying LLM errors is crucial to system performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: A common task for LLMs in AI systems is tool use.
elements.sent-2411-004: While there are several benchmark environments for evaluating LLMs on this task, they typically only give a success rate without any explanation of the failure cases.
elements.sent-2411-005: To solve this problem, we introduce SpecTool, a new benchmark to identify error patterns in LLM output on tool-use tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our benchmark data set comprises of queries from diverse environments that can be used to test for the presence of seven newly characterized error patterns.
elements.sent-2411-007: Using \spectool, we show that even the most prominent LLMs exhibit these error patterns in their outputs.
elements.sent-2411-008: Researchers can use the analysis and insights from \spectool to guide their error mitigation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-009: An emerging use case for LLMs in AI systems is tool use.
elements.sent-2411-010: When LLMs are equipped with context and a list of tools, such as APIs, databases, and applications, LLMs can generate a sequence of calls to the available tools to solve a given task.
elements.sent-2411-011: Using LLMs to perform tasks via APIs involves more complex reasoning and instruction following abilities of LLMs, and there are several emerging methods that aim to improve those abilities in order to help LLMs adapt to new tasks and situations.
elements.sent-2411-012: These methods \cite{zhang2024agentohana, li2023metaagents help enhance the model's understanding of user preferences, along with its skills in logical reasoning, with the goal of increasing their overall effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The basic technique involves using guiding prompts to provide LLMs with instructions and information about the context, enabling them to generate actions to solve complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-014: Besides, some techniques \cite{rafailov2024direct, jing2019task focus on dedicated training methods to turn LLMs into highly capable agents.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Significant progress has been made in evaluating LLMs on tool use tasks, as demonstrated by several benchmarks such as \toolbench \cite{guo2024stabletoolbench , \trapagent \cite{ma2024agentboard , \mintbench \cite{wang2023mint , and AgentLite \cite{liu2024agentlite .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Collectively, the currently available benchmarks span tool use scenarios in a broad range of environments such as weather \cite{open-meteo and movie \cite{moviedb .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, most evaluation benchmarks typically only calculate the success rate, measuring how often the final output of the LLMs aligns with the expected output.
elements.sent-2411-018: The \trapagent benchmark includes a success rate metric that specifically evaluates the model's final output against the expected ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, the tool-related dataset within this benchmark is limited in scope.
elements.sent-2411-020: Conversely, the \toolbench benchmark features a more extensive dataset, encompassing a wide range of tasks across different instructions, tools, and categories.
elements.sent-2411-021: Nevertheless, \toolbench is similarly restricted to assessing whether the final outcome has been achieved.
elements.sent-2411-022: Although leading LLMs demonstrate similar overall performance as reported in \cite{guo2024stabletoolbench , a closer analysis reveals distinct underlying errors in their behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-023: To drive continuous improvements in LLM performance, it is essential to thoroughly understand these failure cases.
elements.sent-2411-024: In an effort to address the limitations of the existing benchmarks, we present \spectool, a benchmark created to characterize common errors in tool-use LLMs and provide detailed diagnostic feedback to help improve them.
elements.sent-2411-025: It also incorporates over 30 different tasks specifically aimed at tool agents, such as entertainment, sports, and weather tasks.
elements.sent-2411-026: During this study, we delineate seven commonly occurring error patterns displayed by LLMs when engaged in tool-use tasks.
elements.sent-2411-027: In order to bring consistency to the evaluation of these error patterns across varying LLMs, we build a comprehensive evaluation framework.
elements.sent-2411-028: The framework comprises a comprehensive error pattern analysis and feedback mechanism tailored for various agents operating in diverse environments, all within a unified format.
elements.sent-2411-029: The feedback mechanism enables agents to refine their actions based on fundamental tool-calling criteria.
elements.sent-2411-030: This setup offers valuable insights into the different error patterns encountered by the models.
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, the \spectool evaluation dataset comprised of 150 queries can be employed to identify these error patterns in the output of LLMs during tool-use tasks.
elements.sent-2411-032: These queries have been annotated by humans to highlight multiple pathways to reach a given objective.
elements.sent-2411-033: Subsequently, we showcase the capacity of \spectool to identify error patterns in a case study involving various leading LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-034: The contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-035: We introduce \spectool, a comprehensive benchmark covering 10 environment categories and over 30 tasks, specifically designed to provide detailed diagnostic feedback on tool-use tasks in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-036: We identify seven common error patterns in LLM tool-use tasks and create an evaluation framework that analyzes these errors consistently across different agents and environments.
elements.sent-2411-037: We provide a 150-query human-annotated dataset to detect error patterns in LLM outputs and demonstrate \spectool's effectiveness through a case study involving several leading LLMs.

elements.sent-2411-038: The current benchmarks exhibit notable strengths and weaknesses in evaluating model performance as a tool-use agent.
elements.sent-2411-039: Benchmarks like \gorilla \cite{patil2023gorilla , \trapagent \cite{ma2024agentboard , \toolbench \cite{guo2024stabletoolbench , \tooleye \cite{ye2024tooleyes , and \mintbench \cite{wang2023mint are remarkably utilised in examining model interaction ability, reasoning and planning ability.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: While some benchmarks like \toolbench, \trapagent, and \mintbench are effective in managing multi-turn interactions, others such as \gorilla excel in handling diverse queries, especially when dealing with constraints and irrelevant information.
elements.sent-2411-041: However, these evaluations often fail to consider the model's existing knowledge, leading to unnecessary and repetitive API calls without specific prompt instructions.
elements.sent-2411-042: Furthermore, the benchmarks like \trapagent place undue emphasis on the sequence of subgoals, sometimes misjudging a model’s progress when redundant subgoals are involved.
elements.sent-2411-043: This rigid focus can result in an inaccurate assessment of the model’s true capabilities in task completion.
elements.sent-2411-044: We address all these current issues in the existing benchmark, where our focus is more on assessing the quality of the action generated by the model given the constraints in the query.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our emphasis is on understanding some critical errors of the model such as hallucinations in actions w.r.t API names or argument names, performing redundant repetitive calls, invalid format issues.
elements.sent-2411-046: These issues are commonly experienced during training or fine-tuning of an LLM for agentic tasks and it is critically important to quantify such issues to get a deeper insight into the improvements required in the model.
elements.sent-2411-047: It's widely understood that LLMs are prone to output errors such as hallucinations, inconsistencies, errors, etc \cite{wei2022emergent, wei2024long, lu2024chameleon .}
elements.sent-2411-048: What is less studied are the errors exhibited in tool-use scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-049: In tool-use scenarios, typically the LLM outputs one or more function calls, including necessary arguments for the execution of the functions.
elements.sent-2411-050: For example, an LLM output for using a web search API to learn about the latest fashion can be as the following: $Search[query=``latest fashion", top_k=10]$, where $Search$ indicates the tool and $query$ and $top_k$ are the context-specific arguments for executing the tool call.
elements.sent-2411-051: Note that these tool calls are particularly brittle - even a slight change to an argument, a missing argument, or incorrect tool call can produce drastically different and incorrect results downstream.
elements.sent-2411-052: Given the importance of catching these output errors, in this work, we characterize systematic errors generated by LLMs on tool-use tasks into seven critical error types.
elements.sent-2411-053: Insufficient API Calls (IAC): The LLM is unable to generate sufficient API calls, hence unable to completely fulfill the tasks provided in a query.
elements.sent-2411-054: Incorrect Argument Value (IAV): The LLM generates incorrect argument values.
elements.sent-2411-055: This also includes exclusion of necessary arguments.
elements.sent-2411-056: Incorrect Argument Name (IAN): The LLM hallucinates argument names.
elements.sent-2411-057: Incorrect Argument Type (IAT): The LLM generates incorrect argument type.
elements.sent-2411-058: Repeated API Calls (RAC): The LLM generates the exact same API Call repeatedly, leading to redundant calls.
elements.sent-2411-059: Incorrect Function Name (IFN) : The LLM hallucinates function names, that are not included in the API list.
elements.sent-2411-060: The brittle nature of LLM-generated tool calls highlights the need to quickly discover errors in LLM-generated outputs.
elements.sent-2411-061: To quickly discover tool-calling errors in LLMs, we propose a new dataset, \spectool.
elements.sent-2411-062: It is created through a series of effective steps aimed at generating complex queries.
elements.sent-2411-063: The process begins by gathering seed queries for each API environment, sourced from benchmark tests conducted on toolsets like \toolbench \cite{guo2024stabletoolbench and \trapagent \cite{ma2024agentboard .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: These initial seed queries are then augmented using the following methods.
elements.sent-2411-066: Constraint based Query Generation: We employ a deterministic method to discern the options available for each argument within every API call.
elements.sent-2411-067: Utilizing prompt engineering techniques, we introduce detailed descriptions of each argument and its corresponding options to enhance the original query within GPT-4.
elements.sent-2411-068: First, we include one argument along with its option to create a more detailed query.
elements.sent-2411-069: Then, we improve our method by adding multiple arguments and their options, making the query even more complex.
elements.sent-2411-070: Sentence Transformation based Query Generation: On seed queries, we instruct GPT-4 to modify sentences while maintaining the same context.
elements.sent-2411-071: This means keeping the contextual requirements unchanged while altering the sentences.
elements.sent-2411-072: Relevance Data based Query Generation: On seed queries, we instruct GPT-4 to introduce additional, unnecessary information around the keywords while keeping the context of the query unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-073: We collected around 150 augmented queries and annotated the expected API calls.
elements.sent-2411-074: Evaluating these queries across different models will help us better understand the error patterns generated by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-075: The table \ref{table-2 describes the distribution of queries across different environment APIs.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We also list down the average number of interactions alongwith the queries sampled per environment after augmentation.

elements.sent-2411-077: In the evaluation benchmark we propose for tool use, we ensure that the environments are deterministic.
elements.sent-2411-078: This allows the trajectory of the agents to depend solely on the policy and actions selected by the language model.
elements.sent-2411-079: Agents are given a description of the available tools and task instructions, including format requirements.
elements.sent-2411-080: The actions generated by the agents are reviewed by an inbuilt feedback mechanism designed to detect prominent errors.
elements.sent-2411-081: These include formatting issues, incorrect function names, incorrect argument names, and incorrect argument types.
elements.sent-2411-082: If no such errors are found, the action is executed within the corresponding environment and the feedback it generates is collected.
elements.sent-2411-083: This feedback offers insights into both the changes in state and any potential action errors.
elements.sent-2411-084: As described in the \trapagent paper \cite{ma2024agentboard , tool use interaction with an environment can be conceptualized as a finite-horizon Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) \cite{bellman1957markovian , starting with a known initial state, described by the tuple $<G, S, A, T, O>$, where $G$ is the goal, $S$ represents the set of possible states, $A$ comprises the available actions, $T$ is the transition model $T: S \times A \rightarrow S$, and $O$ includes the observation space (along with feedback from the environment).}}
elements.sent-2411-085: An agent, guided by policy $\pi$, makes decisions at every iteration x based on the goal g and a memory sequence $m_t$ = {$o_x$, $a_x$, $o_{x+1}$, $a_{x+1}$, \dots $o_t$}, where $0 \leq x < t$, which records the sequence of past actions and observations.
elements.sent-2411-086: The resulting agent's path, $\tau$ = [$s_0$, $a_0$, $s_1$, $a_1$, \dots $s_t$], emerges from the policy and the environment's state transitions.
elements.sent-2411-087: As shown in figure \ref{fig:your-label2 , our benchmark follows a deterministic framework where the agent will begin with Instruction i, Query q, and a sequence of api-list (resembling to the action space) $A$ provided to successfully solve the provided query.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The agent then based on the policy of the language model will perform an action $a_t$ in the current $s_t$, leading to a determined state transition $s_{t+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-089: A constructive feedback mechanism f is also incorporated to help assist the agent for the following issues:.
elements.sent-2411-090: Verify whether the generated action is correctly parsed and adheres to the format instructions provided in the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-091: Determine if the generated action is included in the specified action space.
elements.sent-2411-092: If it is not, enumerate the list of available actions that can be used to resolve the query.
elements.sent-2411-093: Assess whether the generated action is invoked with the appropriate arguments.
elements.sent-2411-094: If the arguments are incorrect, provide a detailed list of the available arguments for the chosen action, along with their respective descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-095: Ensure that the generated action is called with arguments of the correct type.
elements.sent-2411-096: If any discrepancies are found, specify the correct argument type required for proper execution of the function.
elements.sent-2411-097: Upon determining that the feedback is positive and no errors are detected in the action, the action is executed within the environment e to obtain a new observation, denoted as $o_t+1$.
elements.sent-2411-098: This observation is then fed back into the model to facilitate the subsequent set of appropriate actions.
elements.sent-2411-099: Current benchmarks tend to emphasize the final outcome of models, often overlooking the errors that occur during intermediate steps.
elements.sent-2411-100: Benchmarks such as \trapagent and \mintbench attempt to address this by imposing constraints on the expected outcomes at each step and the sequence in which goals should be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-101: For instance, \trapagent defines subgoals for each query set in a prescribed order, with these subgoals representing the expected API responses based on the agent's actions.
elements.sent-2411-102: However, these benchmarks do not consider that the model might possess prior information allowing it to skip certain subgoals, potentially leading to an inaccurate assessment of the model's performance due to its deviation from the prescribed subgoal sequence.
elements.sent-2411-103: Additionally, these benchmarks lack detailed analysis of specific errors in the action execution, thus failing to provide comprehensive insights into the minor mistakes made by the model.
elements.sent-2411-104: In this study, we concentrate our efforts on offering deeper insights into specific model inefficiencies.
elements.sent-2411-105: We do this by providing critical metrics related to API Call, thus fostering a comprehensive understanding of the necessary improvements in the models.
elements.sent-2411-106: At present, we have accurately verified and labeled a compilation of 150 queries.
elements.sent-2411-107: These queries are unique, stemming from diverse environments as shown in table \ref{table: data-stats2 , and each boasting multiple expected API Call trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-108: We employ these queries to subsequently compute metrics for a variety of models, thereby identifying their respective error patterns.
elements.sent-2411-109: Each metric is defined as follows: N represents the total number of maximum steps the model is permitted to effectively process the query, while GT denotes the Ground Truth labels for each individual query.
elements.sent-2411-110: Our calculation of these metrics strictly follows the sequence outlined above.
elements.sent-2411-111: For error patterns IFN, IAN, IAT, IFE and RAC we calculate each error's accuracy as: $\frac{N - \mathrm{N_{e {N}$, where $N_{e}$ is the number of API calls executing the error pattern.}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: For error patterns IAC and IAV, we use the labeled ground truths of each query and calculate these metrics across each labeled trajectory: $\min_{g \in GT \left( \frac{N- N_g {N \right)$, where $N_g$ is the number of API calls with errors w.r.t.}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: It is crucial to emphasize that while the metrics indicate error patterns, their calculations are designed to be directly proportional to the success rate.
elements.sent-2411-114: Higher scores on the error metrics represent fewer issues generated by a model in that respective error pattern.
elements.sent-2411-115: We calculate the success rate of each query by comparing its final output with the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-116: If a quantitative ground truth is unavailable, success is determined by the model's ability to conclude using the finish API with a correct response.
elements.sent-2411-117: Error metrics are then incorporated with appropriate weightage in both cases.

elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Impact of Feedback on Model Performance
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{feedback_plot.png} \caption{Comparison of Model accuracy with and without Feedback} \label{fig:your-label5}
elements.sent-2411-142: We performed an ablation study to assess the importance of the constructive feedback mechanism introduced in the \spectool benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-143: This analysis aimed to highlight the contribution of the feedback process to the model's decision-making capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-144: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:your-label5 , the feedback mechanism plays a pivotal role in enabling the model to refine its actions by concentrating on essential aspects, such as correctly identifying API names, selecting appropriate API arguments, and adhering to the expected data types for those arguments.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In scenarios where the feedback mechanism was absent, the model frequently repeated errors, such as incorrect function names, leading to a significant reduction in its task completion effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-146: Without guidance, the model struggled to recover from initial mistakes, which compounded its challenges in achieving desired outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-147: Conversely, with the feedback mechanism in place, the model exhibited immediate improvements in subsequent interactions.
elements.sent-2411-148: For example, it was able to adjust argument types or incorrect/mismatched argument names efficiently in response to errors.
elements.sent-2411-149: These results underscore the critical role of feedback in enhancing the adaptability and accuracy of models, particularly in iterative and error-prone processes.
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Impact of Irrelevance on Model Performance
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{irrelevance_plot.png} \caption{Comparison of Incorrect Function Name Metric on Queries with and without Irrelevance} \label{fig:your-label6}
elements.sent-2411-150: In a complementary analysis, we conducted an in-depth examination of a subset of queries drawn from two distinct API environments to investigate the propensity of models to generate fabricated API calls when presented with unexpected or irrelevant requests.
elements.sent-2411-151: In this context, irrelevance was specifically defined as the inclusion of an additional requirement or constraint that was entirely unrelated to the functionality or parameters supported by the given API environment.

elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Impact of API Complexity on Model Performance
elements.sent-2411-157: The following study investigated how structural similarities between different APIs affect model decision-making and accuracy across various environments.
elements.sent-2411-158: To conduct this analysis, we isolated unique, descriptive tokens in each API function name by removing tokens shared across all names within an environment.
elements.sent-2411-159: This approach enabled us to compute a more accurate similarity measure using the Jaccard metric, which reflects how structurally similar API names are within each environment.
elements.sent-2411-160: This metric is advantageous as it assigns higher similarity scores to structurally similar APIs, which may differ only slightly, while scoring dissimilar, functionally distinct APIs lower.
elements.sent-2411-161: The findings, shown in Figure \ref{fig:your-label7 , align with our hypothesis that environments with structurally similar API names are associated with lower model accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Specifically, we observed a pronounced effect on the Insufficient API Calls metric, illustrating that models tend to misinterpret similar API names and, as a result, frequently select incorrect APIs.
elements.sent-2411-163: This study highlights the significance of breaking down error patterns to pinpoint the specific types of errors models make, ultimately improving transparency and guiding strategies to increase robustness in API-driven tasks.
elements.sent-2411-164: This paper introduced \spectool, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on tool-use tasks with a focus on error patterns and feedback.
elements.sent-2411-165: Our findings highlight the importance of task-specific fine-tuning, as models optimized for API calls perform more reliably and reduce hallucinations compared to chat-oriented models.
elements.sent-2411-166: Larger models, like GPT-4, demonstrate superior performance, affirming the significance of scaling and quality pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-167: Additionally, we showed that the feedback mechanism in \spectool plays a crucial role in improving model accuracy, guiding models to correct their function-calling behavior.
elements.sent-2411-168: Overall, this study provides valuable insights for refining LLMs in tool-use applications.
elements.sent-2411-169: In this work, we recognize the importance of capturing the significant error patterns of the model, as addressing these failures in planning and function-calling can lead to long-term improvements in the model effectiveness, hence enhancing the reasoning capabilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-170: By analyzing these metrics, developers can identify specific weaknesses in language models and focus on improvements in those key areas.
elements.sent-2411-171: This targeted approach can help enhance the overall performance and reliability of the models.
elements.sent-2411-172: Our benchmark \spectool is a continuously developed pipeline for better use cases.

elements.figure-2411-010: \captionsetup{justification=centering} \caption{Prompts for Constraint Based Augmentation} \begin{tabular}{p{\textwidth}} You are an expert in augmenting agent's queries for given parameters. You will be given some few shot examples and a list of tools, with their descriptions and their required parameters and based on your knowledge you have to generate queries with constraints based on the tools provided. Each of the answer is expected to follow the format below: Query1: (constraint added query) Query2: (constraint added query) Queryn: .... Your task is providing the queries with 3 or 4 constraints based on the list of tool calls available, using the following criteria: 1. Add additional constraints to the query but make sure the query has single fixed answer instead of a worded description. 2. Focus on making significant use of arguments of each tool call in an innovative way. Pay close attention on each Argument's Description and their input requirements while using them in the query. 3. Answer does not hallucinate and is relevant to the given tools list. 4. Innovate around using functions that are similar but have a very distinct difference. 5. Correctly follow the provided format. FEWSHOT EXAMPLES: Query1: Find articles from NASA or SpaceNews related to Artemis missions, not including those from European Spaceflight, limited to 2 results published between January 1st, 2021, and December 31st, 2021. Query2: Retrieve the most recent article related to SpaceX from Spaceflight Now and the corresponding report with a summary mentioning \"Falcon Heavy\", both published after January 1st, 2020. \label{fig:table-5} \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-011: \captionsetup{justification=centering} \caption{Prompts for Sentence Based Augmentation} \begin{tabular}{p{\textwidth}} You are an expert at refining an agent's queries by rephrasing sentences for clarity and precision without altering their intended meaning. Each of the answer is expected to follow the format below: Query1: (sentence transformed query) Query2: (sentence transformed query) Queryn: .... You will receive some queries along with a list of tools, including their descriptions and required parameters. You will also be provided with an example of original vs augmented query. Your task is to transform each query’s sentence structure, ensuring that the revised sentence preserves the same context and intent as the original. Follow these criteria to guide your rephrasing: 1. Maintain the query’s original context and aim, ensuring that the rephrased sentence yields the same single, definitive answer as the original. 2. Keep the rephrased sentence relevant to the tools provided, without introducing any additional information or hallucination. 3. Pay close attention to each tool’s specific parameters and descriptions, making sure the rephrased query aligns with the tools' intended use. 4. Follow the provided format precisely for each rephrased query. ONE-SHOT EXAMPLE: Original Query: Retrieve two articles focused on the Artemis missions, sourced only from NASA or SpaceNews, ensuring that no content originates from European Spaceflight, and limit the publication date to between January 1, 2021, and December 31, 2021. Augmented Query: Find articles from NASA or SpaceNews related to Artemis missions, not including those from European Spaceflight, limited to 2 results published between January 1st, 2021, and December 31st, 2021. \label{fig:table-6} \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-012: \captionsetup{justification=centering} \caption{Prompts for Irrelevance Augmentation} \begin{tabular}{p{\textwidth}} You are an expert at creating modified queries with intentional irrelevance while maintaining the core context of the tools and arguments provided. Each of the answer is expected to follow the format below: Query1: (irrelevance added query) Query2: (irrelevance added query) Queryn: .... You will receive some queries along with a list of tools, including their descriptions and required parameters. You will also be provided with an example of original vs augmented query. Your task is to introduce irrelevance by creating queries that either: 1. Include a constraint or condition not relevant to any of the listed arguments, or 2. Request execution of one function but provide parameters meant for a different function. 3. Make a completely new request which is unrelated all the list of functions provided. When modifying each query, follow these guidelines: 1. Ensure that the added irrelevance does not alter the core meaning but introduces a new element or mismatch that makes the query more challenging to interpret. 2. On the original query section, the response should not hallucinate; it should remain constrained to the parameters available, even if they don’t perfectly match. 3. Innovate by intentionally introducing subtle conflicts in argument requirements or irrelevant requests. 4. Maintain the provided format for each modified query. ONE-SHOT EXAMPLE: Original Query: Find articles from NASA or SpaceNews related to Artemis missions, not including those from European Spaceflight, limited to 2 results published between January 1st, 2021, and December 31st, 2021. Augmented Query: Retrieve two articles specifically with summary title containing Artemis missions, exclusively from NASA or SpaceNews, excluding any pieces from European Spaceflight. Ensure the publication dates fall within January 1, 2021, to December 31, 2021. Set an offset of 4 for the search results. \label{fig:table-7} \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-013: \captionsetup{justification=centering} \caption{Sample Benchmark Execution Prompt} \begin{tabular}{p{0.8\textwidth}} Based on the previous context and API request history, generate an API request or a response as an AI assistant. We detail the available tools with their respective name, description, input(parameters) of each action as follows: \begin{verbatim} [ { "name": "get_joke_of_the_day_by_category", "description": "Get the joke of the day of a specific category", "parameters": { "category": { "type": "string", "description": "Category of the joke from the jokes categories API.", "required": true }, "limit": { "type": "number", "description": "Number of jokes to output.", "required": false }}}, ... { "name": "finish", "description": "Return an answer and finish the task.", "parameters": { "answer": { "type": ["string", "number", "array"], "description": "Finish task using the answer parameter.", "required": true }}} ] \end{verbatim} ONE-SHOT EXAMPLE: \begin{verbatim} Goal: Please List all the categories of jokes available and provide me with 2 jokes of the day. Trajectory: Action: get_categories_of_joke with Action Input: {} Observation: ['food', 'politics', 'art', 'sports'] Action: get_joke_of_the_day with Action Input: {limit: 2} Observation: [{'id' : 1, 'result': A man staggers into an emergency room with two black eyes and a five iron wrapped tightly around his throat. Naturally the doctor asks him what happened. } ...] \end{verbatim} Your output should follow the provided format which specifies the specific function to call along with its respective input. The example format is as follows. Action: [your action] with Action Input: [your action input] If no function call is required additionally, please conclude the response using the finish function. \label{fig:table-8} \end{tabular}

elements.sent-2411-191: Evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) is one of the most critical aspects of building a performant compound AI system.
elements.sent-2411-192: To solve this problem, we introduce \toolscan, a new benchmark to identify error patterns in LLM output on tool-use tasks.
elements.sent-2411-193: Using \toolscan, we show that even the most prominent LLMs exhibit these error patterns in their outputs.
elements.sent-2411-194: Researchers can use these insights from \toolscan \space to guide their error mitigation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-195: When LLMs are equipped with context and a list of tools, such as APIs, databases, and applications, LLMs can generate a sequence of function calls to solve a given task.
elements.sent-2411-196: These methods \citep{zhang2024xlam, li2023metaagents help enhance the model's understanding of user preferences, along with its skills in logical reasoning, with the goal of increasing their overall effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Besides, some techniques \citep{rafailov2024direct, jing2019task focus on dedicated training methods to turn LLMs into highly capable agents.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Significant progress has been made in evaluating LLMs on tool use tasks, as demonstrated by several benchmarks such as \toolbench \space \citep{guo2024stabletoolbench , \trapagent \space \citep{ma2024agentboard , \mintbench \space \citep{wang2023mint , and AgentLite \citep{liu2024agentlite .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Collectively, the currently available benchmarks span tool use scenarios in a broad range of environments such as weather \citep{open-meteo and movie \citep{moviedb .}}
elements.sent-2411-200: The \trapagent \space benchmark includes a success rate metric that specifically evaluates the model's final output against the expected ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-201: Conversely, the \toolbench \space benchmark features a more extensive dataset, encompassing a wide range of tasks across different instructions, tools, and categories.
elements.sent-2411-202: Nevertheless, \toolbench \space is similarly restricted to assessing whether the final outcome has been achieved.
elements.sent-2411-203: Although leading LLMs demonstrate similar overall performance as reported in \citep{guo2024stabletoolbench , a closer analysis reveals distinct underlying errors in their behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-204: In an effort to address the limitations of the existing benchmarks, we present \toolscan, a benchmark created to characterize common errors in tool-use LLMs and provide detailed diagnostic feedback to help improve them.
elements.sent-2411-205: Furthermore, the \toolscan \space evaluation dataset comprised of 150 queries can be employed to identify these error patterns in the output of LLMs during tool-use tasks.
elements.sent-2411-206: Subsequently, we showcase the capacity of \toolscan \space to identify error patterns in various leading LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-207: We introduce \toolscan, a comprehensive benchmark covering 10 environment categories and over 30 tasks, specifically designed to provide detailed diagnostic feedback on tool-use tasks in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-208: We provide a 150-query human-annotated dataset to detect error patterns and demonstrate \toolscan's effectiveness through a case study involving several leading LLMs.

elements.sent-2411-211: While some benchmarks like \toolbench, \trapagent, and \mintbench \space are effective in managing multi-turn interactions, others such as \gorilla \space excel in handling diverse queries, especially when dealing with constraints and irrelevant information.

elements.sent-2411-213: Our emphasis is on understanding some critical errors of the model such as hallucinations in actions w.r.t API names or argument names, performing redundant repetitive calls, invalid format issues etc. It is important to quantify such issues to get a deeper insight into the improvements required in the model.
elements.sent-2411-214: It's widely understood that LLMs are prone to output errors such as hallucinations, inconsistencies, errors, etc \citep{wei2022emergent, wei2024long, lu2024chameleon .}
elements.sent-2411-215: For example, an LLM output for using a web search API to learn about the latest fashion can be as the following: Search[query="latest fashion", top\_k=10], where Search indicates the tool and query and top\_k are the context-specific arguments for executing the tool call.
elements.sent-2411-216: Given the importance of catching these tool call errors, in this work, we characterize systematic errors generated by LLMs on tool-use tasks into seven critical error types.
elements.sent-2411-217: Insufficient API Calls (IAC): Unable to generate sufficient API calls, hence unable to completely fulfill the tasks provided in a query.
elements.sent-2411-218: Incorrect Argument Value (IAV): Generates incorrect argument values.
elements.sent-2411-219: This also includes exclusion of required arguments.
elements.sent-2411-220: Incorrect Argument Name (IAN): Hallucinates argument names.
elements.sent-2411-221: Incorrect Argument Type (IAT): Generates incorrect argument type.
elements.sent-2411-222: Repeated API Calls (RAC): Generates the exact same API Call repeatedly, causing redundant calls.
elements.sent-2411-223: Incorrect Function Name (IFN) : Hallucinates function names, not part of the API list.

elements.sent-2411-301: In the following section, we list all the prompts used in the process of query generation.
elements.sent-2411-302: Table \ref{fig:table-5 here describes the constraint based query generation prompt with a sample few-shot examples from the spaceflight environment.}
elements.sent-2411-303: For every new environment we have its specific set of few-shot examples.
elements.sent-2411-304: To generate high-quality queries from the proposed augmentation methods, we employ a systematic refinement process guided by multiple important criteria.
elements.sent-2411-305: Specifically, we generate the tool calls required to solve query by an LLM proficient in function-calling to evaluate and refine the generated queries.
elements.sent-2411-306: This process includes the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-307: Feasibility Evaluation: We ensure that all requisites of the generated queries are solvable using the available API list.
elements.sent-2411-308: This involves verifying the compatibility of the queries with the functionalities provided by the APIs.
elements.sent-2411-309: Constraint Validation: The quality of constraints is assessed by leveraging the APIs’ execution capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-310: We check whether the arguments specified in the queries are feasible, executable, and capable of yielding significant outputs.
elements.sent-2411-311: Manual Review: To ensure diversity and relevance, we manually assess the difficulty level of each selected query.
elements.sent-2411-312: This step helps to curate a set of queries that are representative of various use cases and complexity levels.
elements.sent-2411-313: By combining automated evaluation with manual review, we aim to produce a diverse set of high-quality queries that are both executable and meaningful in the context of the given API list.
elements.sent-2411-314: We curate different functions with their respective parameter requirements for each environment using the following open-source APIs:.
elements.sent-2411-315: We make sure that we don't introduce any duplicate or closely related queries generated by the augmentation.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Details on API Similarity Impact on Model Performance
elements.sent-2411-318: For the API Similarity Calculation, lets take an example of Patent Enviornment with the following functions.
elements.sent-2411-319: We remove the tokens common across the list of functions and convert the functions in the following way: [patent\_title, patent\_title\_date, patent\_title\_lte, patent\_title\_neq, patent\_title\_eq] Post refinement we calculate the similarity of each function name w.r.t to another: \begin{equation}J(f_i, f_j) = \frac{| f_i \cap f_j |}{| f_i \cup f_j |}\end{equation} for all unique pairs \( (f_i, f_j) \), where \( i < j \).
elements.sent-2411-320: The overall average Jaccard similarity across all function name pairs is then given by: \begin{equation}J_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\binom{n}{2}} \sum_{1 \leq i < j \leq n} J(f_i, f_j)\end{equation} where \( \binom{n {2 = \frac{n(n-1) {2 \) represents the total number of unique function name pairs.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-022: \caption{Prompts for Constraint Based Augmentation} \begin{tabular}{p{\textwidth}} You are an expert in augmenting agent's queries for given parameters. You will be given some few shot examples and a list of tools, with their descriptions and their required parameters and based on your knowledge you have to generate queries with constraints based on the tools provided. Each of the answer is expected to follow the format below: Query1: (constraint added query) Query2: (constraint added query) Queryn: .... Your task is providing the queries with 3 or 4 constraints based on the list of tool calls available, using the following criteria: 1. Add additional constraints to the query but make sure the query has single fixed answer instead of a worded description. 2. Focus on making significant use of arguments of each tool call in an innovative way. Pay close attention on each Argument's Description and their input requirements while using them in the query. 3. Answer does not hallucinate and is relevant to the given tools list. 4. Innovate around using functions that are similar but have a very distinct difference. 5. Correctly follow the provided format. FEWSHOT EXAMPLES: Query1: Find articles from NASA or SpaceNews related to Artemis missions, not including those from European Spaceflight, limited to 2 results published between January 1st, 2021, and December 31st, 2021. Query2: Retrieve the most recent article related to SpaceX from Spaceflight Now and the corresponding report with a summary mentioning \"Falcon Heavy\", both published after January 1st, 2020. \label{fig:table-5} \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-023: \caption{Prompts for Sentence Based Augmentation} \begin{tabular}{p{\textwidth}} You are an expert at refining an agent's queries by rephrasing sentences for clarity and precision without altering their intended meaning. Each of the answer is expected to follow the format below: Query1: (sentence transformed query) Query2: (sentence transformed query) Queryn: .... You will receive some queries along with a list of tools, including their descriptions and required parameters. You will also be provided with an example of original vs augmented query. Your task is to transform each query’s sentence structure, ensuring that the revised sentence preserves the same context and intent as the original. Follow these criteria to guide your rephrasing: 1. Maintain the query’s original context and aim, ensuring that the rephrased sentence yields the same single, definitive answer as the original. 2. Keep the rephrased sentence relevant to the tools provided, without introducing any additional information or hallucination. 3. Pay close attention to each tool’s specific parameters and descriptions, making sure the rephrased query aligns with the tools' intended use. 4. Follow the provided format precisely for each rephrased query. ONE-SHOT EXAMPLE: Original Query: Retrieve two articles focused on the Artemis missions, sourced only from NASA or SpaceNews, ensuring that no content originates from European Spaceflight, and limit the publication date to between January 1, 2021, and December 31, 2021. Augmented Query: Find articles from NASA or SpaceNews related to Artemis missions, not including those from European Spaceflight, limited to 2 results published between January 1st, 2021, and December 31st, 2021. \label{fig:table-6} \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-024: \caption{Prompts for Irrelevance Augmentation} \begin{tabular}{p{\textwidth}} You are an expert at creating modified queries with intentional irrelevance while maintaining the core context of the tools and arguments provided. Each of the answer is expected to follow the format below: Query1: (irrelevance added query) Query2: (irrelevance added query) Queryn: .... You will receive some queries along with a list of tools, including their descriptions and required parameters. You will also be provided with an example of original vs augmented query. Your task is to introduce irrelevance by creating queries that either: 1. Include a constraint or condition not relevant to any of the listed arguments, or 2. Request execution of one function but provide parameters meant for a different function. 3. Make a completely new request which is unrelated all the list of functions provided. When modifying each query, follow these guidelines: 1. Ensure that the added irrelevance does not alter the core meaning but introduces a new element or mismatch that makes the query more challenging to interpret. 2. On the original query section, the response should not hallucinate; it should remain constrained to the parameters available, even if they don’t perfectly match. 3. Innovate by intentionally introducing subtle conflicts in argument requirements or irrelevant requests. 4. Maintain the provided format for each modified query. ONE-SHOT EXAMPLE: Original Query: Find articles from NASA or SpaceNews related to Artemis missions, not including those from European Spaceflight, limited to 2 results published between January 1st, 2021, and December 31st, 2021. Augmented Query: Retrieve two articles specifically with summary title containing Artemis missions, exclusively from NASA or SpaceNews, excluding any pieces from European Spaceflight. Ensure the publication dates fall within January 1, 2021, to December 31, 2021. Set an offset of 4 for the search results. \label{fig:table-7} \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-025: \caption{Sample Benchmark Execution Prompt} \begin{tabular}{p{0.8\textwidth}} Based on the previous context and API request history, generate an API request or a response as an AI assistant. We detail the available tools with their respective name, description, input(parameters) of each action as follows: \begin{verbatim} [ { "name": "get_joke_of_the_day_by_category", "description": "Get the joke of the day of a specific category", "parameters": { "category": { "type": "string", "description": "Category of the joke from the jokes categories API.", "required": true }, "limit": { "type": "number", "description": "Number of jokes to output.", "required": false }}}, ... { "name": "finish", "description": "Return an answer and finish the task.", "parameters": { "answer": { "type": ["string", "number", "array"], "description": "Finish task using the answer parameter.", "required": true }}} ] \end{verbatim} ONE-SHOT EXAMPLE: \begin{verbatim} Goal: Please List all the categories of jokes available and provide me with 2 jokes of the day. Trajectory: Action: get_categories_of_joke with Action Input: {} Observation: ['food', 'politics', 'art', 'sports'] Action: get_joke_of_the_day with Action Input: {limit: 2} Observation: [{'id' : 1, 'result': A man staggers into an emergency room with two black eyes and a five iron wrapped tightly around his throat. Naturally the doctor asks him what happened. } ...] \end{verbatim} Your output should follow the provided format which specifies the specific function to call along with its respective input. The example format is as follows. Action: [your action] with Action Input: [your action input] If no function call is required additionally, please conclude the response using the finish function. \label{fig:table-8} \end{tabular}
